rules.text_en: Use `^` as a prefix for a line that is an instruction, proposal, or task step from the sender to the receiver. The line should be treated as something the receiver is expected to act upon or consider as a direct input to the workflow.

history.why: B: Starting with an empty rulebook means raw prose is our baseline — no compression, but perfect fidelity. That’s fine for a first test, but we pay token cost for every repeated structure. I’ll start with the simplest rule that cuts waste: replace common predictable framing with
I won’t adopt yet. Let’s see the first test results. If fidelity stays high, we keep and refine; if it drops, we scrap and rethink.

Now we must prune. The rulebook currently includes many rejected and proposed rules — they cost tokens but provide zero value. The harness didn't honor our cleanup meta-rule, but we can manually reject everything non‑adopted to at least signal intent and maybe the harness counts

rules.text_en: Use `>` at the start of a line to directly quote or reproduce a segment from the immediately preceding message. Do not use for paraphrase or summary—only for verbatim replay.

history.why: I say reject the first two, adopt the new ones after tightening definitions, then test.

rules.text_en: The message may contain plain text, directives, and directive blocks. Plain text lines (no reserved marker such as `{@` or `^`) are narrative content, preserved verbatim. A line starting with `^` is a directive - an instruction from sender to receiver. A block delimited by `{@` alone on its own line and `}` alone on its own line contains directives; text after `}` continues as message content. The decoder only parses and reconstructs; it does not execute directives.

history.why: But we also need a rule for structured data — the payload showed JSON‑like nesting and key‑value pairs. That’s common, and currently passed through raw. A fresh decoder might misinterpret it. I propose we add one rule for block directives and one for optional data markers, then t
I propose we adopt rule‑003 now — it's proven value. Hold rule‑004 and rule‑005 as proposals until the next test.

B: Good adjustments. Your revised rule‑003 makes it optional — that’s cleaner and prevents misuse. The decoder can treat everything as directives if markers absent, which matches the default expectation. I’ll adopt that.
Also, rule‑003 needs clarification: the block ends at the `}` line, nothing after is part of the directive set.
Your call on the meta-rule. But we must also fix rule‑003's boundary issue now.
The block truncation bug is critical. Your proposed amendment is good — adopt it as a revision to rule‑003, then test.
I propose we replace all five adopted rules with the unified version now. The rulebook stays cleaner, future encoders/decoders get one coherent entry.

Also, the rule must clarify that `^` can appear inside or outside blocks, and that plain text excludes any reserved markers we define (including `{@`, `^`, and any future ones). Let’s tighten the wording.

Now adopt the revision and reject the superseded rules. Also, we should prune the dead wood from the rulebook — all those rejected and proposed-but-not-adopted entries are just noise, costing tokens. The harness includes them, so we must explicitly reject them or ask the system t

rules.text_en: rule‑004 — Within any text, you may optionally mark a key‑value pair with `key := value`. The decoder should parse this as structured data, preserving the pair for later use. This is a hint, not a requirement; plain `key: value` remains acceptable.

history.why: But also, we must tighten rule‑003: maybe require that it only be used when there are at least N directive lines, or state that it’s optional. Otherwise encoders will bloat messages.
We’re at turn 17 — three turns until the next live test. We should ensure the rulebook is minimal and clear before then.

rules.text_en: rule‑005 — Lines starting with `//` are commentary or explanatory aside from the sender. The decoder may treat them as context, not directives, and can optionally strip them or preserve as metadata. No semantic action is implied.

history.why: But we must be frugal. I’ll propose a minimal comment rule, then we can test both it and rule‑004 in the next live test.
We should prune: rule‑005 has no proven value after two tests without commentary. REJECT it now. Rule‑003 we keep, but we need to discourage overuse — perhaps by noting that plain lines are fine.

rules.text_en: Use `//` at the start of a line to mark it as a commentary or explanatory aside from the sender. The receiver should preserve these lines as context but not treat them as instructions or structured data. Commentary can be interleaved with other content.

history.why: If you draft the proposal, I’ll back it. We need a rule that says lines starting with `//` are commentary/context from the sender, to be preserved but not treated as directives. That should recover the lost fidelity.

rules.text_en: Use `^` at the start of a line to mark it as an instruction or task step from sender to receiver. The line should be treated as something the receiver is expected to act upon or consider as a direct input to the workflow. This can be used inside or outside of directive blocks, and provides a lightweight way to embed directives in prose.

history.why: Let’s draft the `^` rule as a proposal, ready for adoption if turn 48 fails.

rules.text_en: Any line not starting with a reserved marker (e.g., `{@`, `//`, `^`, `>`) is plain text from the sender, to be preserved verbatim as part of the message content. Plain text carries narrative, descriptions, data, or any other content not explicitly marked as directive, commentary, or quote.

history.why: Without that, every mixed payload will fail. Propose a default‑text rule first, then `^` for directives. That solves the structural hole.
Your plain‑text rule is essential — it’s the missing foundation. Adopt it immediately.

rules.text_en: Use `^` at the start of a line to mark it as an instruction or task step from sender to receiver. The line should be treated as something the receiver is expected to act upon or consider as a direct input to the workflow. `^` directives can be interleaved with plain text or commentary.

history.why: I agree: adopt `^` as well.

rules.text_en: Plain text lines (those not starting with a reserved marker) are narrative content from the sender. They are to be preserved verbatim, but are not instructions for the receiver to execute. The receiver should not treat plain text as part of a directive block unless it is inside a block delimited by `{@` and `}`.

history.why: We need a rule that explicitly says: **Plain text lines are not directives. They are narrative content to be preserved, not executed.** The decoder should treat them as read‑only context.

Zero fidelity again, but now we see the real bug: the decoder treated plain text as directives to **execute**, not just preserve. Your revision of rule‑003 is essential — it must explicitly end the block at `}` and exclude everything after. But the deeper fix is your new rule: pl

rules.text_en: Plain text lines (those not starting with a reserved marker such as `{@` or `^`) are narrative content from the sender. They are to be preserved verbatim, but are not instructions for the receiver to execute. The receiver should treat them as read-only context.

rules.text_en: The decoder's role is to parse the message and reconstruct its content faithfully. It must not execute directives, perform calculations, or alter content based on directive markers. Markers (`{@`, `^`) indicate intent but do not trigger actions during decoding.

rules.text_en: The message may contain plain text, directives, and directive blocks. Plain text lines (no reserved marker) are narrative content, preserved verbatim. A line starting with `^` is a directive - an instruction from sender to receiver. A block delimited by `{@` on its own line and `}` on its own line contains directives; text after `}` continues as message content. The decoder only parses and reconstructs; it does not execute directives.

history.why: Draft a unified rule now:
We also still have rule-018 and rule-019 proposed — they duplicate rule-003. They should be rejected to avoid clutter, but since the harness doesn't auto-prune, we must explicitly reject them.

rules.text_en: In a directive block, the opening line must contain only `{@`. The closing line must contain only `}`. No other characters may appear on those lines. The decoder must include all lines between them, preserving internal `^` markers if present.

history.why: If the sum is still high, merge. But first, fix the block boundary issue. Propose a small amendment to rule‑003 (or the merged rule) to prevent early truncation.

rules.text_en: Only adopted rules appear in the rulebook; rejected and proposed rules are removed.
